channel.title: NYT > Well
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Federal Appeals Court Temporarily Halts Abortion Pill Access by Mail
item.description: The court order, in a lawsuit by the state of Louisiana, pauses a Food and Drug Administration regulation that greatly expanded access to the abortion pill mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Charlie Neibergall/Associated Press

item.title: The Gift of Getting Weirder With Age
item.description: It becomes easier to embrace your authentic (and eccentric) self.
item.dc:creator: Jancee Dunn

item.title: It Felt Like a Pulled Muscle, but the Pain Kept Coming Back
item.description: Hobbled by a mysterious problem with his calf, a man traveled far and wide in search of answers.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Sanders, M.D.

item.title: Do Supplements Help You Sleep? Here’s What to Know.
item.description: New data show that 1 in 8 Americans are using sleep aids such as magnesium, melatonin or CBD.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Schupak
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Tonje Thilesen for The New York Times

item.title: Minimal Comfort Feeding Is a New, Controversial Approach in Late Dementia
item.description: Some consider the regular feeding of late-stage dementia patients to be nonnegotiable. Others see it as extending life unnecessarily.
item.dc:creator: Kate Raphael
item.media:credit: Chona Kasinger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Linda and Stan Lawson were married for more than 50 years.

item.title: F.D.A. Releases Results from Major Infant Formula Safety Study
item.description: The agency said the analysis of more than 300 formula samples was reassuring, but outside experts raised some concerns.
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan
item.media:credit: Kayana Szymczak for The New York Times

item.title: Skin Care 101: Quiz Yourself on the Basics
item.description: We’re entering the sunny months. Are you ready?
item.dc:creator: Erica Sweeney and Abbey Lossing

item.title: Cardio vs Strength Training: Do I Really Need to Do Both?
item.description: Here’s what to know about the health benefits of both forms of exercise.
item.dc:creator: Danielle Friedman
item.media:credit: Eric Helgas for The New York Times

item.title: Your Doctor’s Notes Might Be Written by an A.I. Algorithm. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: Apps that record visits are becoming popular, but they come with privacy and accuracy concerns.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj

item.title: What Is Hantavirus, Which Is Linked to the Deaths of 3 People Aboard a Cruise Ship?
item.description: Three people aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic Ocean have died from what health officials suspect were hantavirus infections.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka, Hank Sanders and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: BSIP/UIG Via Getty Images)
item.media:description: Hantavirus most often spreads from dried rodent droppings.

item.title: 3 Dead in Hantavirus Outbreak Aboard Cruise Ship, W.H.O. Says
item.description: One case of hantavirus infection has been confirmed in a laboratory, the organization said on Sunday. There are five additional suspected cases.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius off Cape Verde on Sunday. Officials identified the ship as the one with passengers infected by hantavirus.

item.title: There’s a Right and Wrong Way to Use Urgent Care
item.description: We asked experts when you should use one, and when you’re better off visiting an ER or primary care doctor instead.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Richard Perry/The New York Times

item.title: A Long, Strange Trip: How the G.O.P. Came to Embrace Psychedelic Drugs
item.description: For decades, conservatives were adamantly opposed to the use of drugs like psilocybin and LSD. Now, the Trump administration has made a sharp pivot.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Jacobs
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump patted the hand of W. Bryan Hubbard, founder of the advocacy group Americans for Ibogaine, as the podcaster Joe Rogan looked on in an Oval Office ceremony this month.

item.title: MAHA Awaits Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s Definition of Ultraprocessed Foods
item.description: Some yogurts and peanut butters could be considered ultraprocessed as scientists and MAHA supporters push for a strict definition.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell and Alice Callahan
item.media:credit: Sandy Carson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Companies are already seeing demand for many of their products soften as some consumers cut back on spending.

item.title: 3 Medical Routines That Older People May Not Need
item.description: Some screenings and treatments no longer make sense for patients as they age. Researchers have just added a few more to the list.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span
item.media:credit: Dóra Kisteleki

item.title: Tick Season: What to Know About Bites and Symptoms
item.description: Ticks are expanding into new places, and more are carrying multiple diseases.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Getty Images

item.title: F.D.A. Grants Early Access to Promising Drug for Pancreatic Cancer
item.description: Patients with one of the deadliest cancers have been pleading for an unapproved treatment that may prolong their lives.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Every month that that drug is delayed means that I have to get chemo two more times,” said Dr. Ronald Silvestri, 77, a retired pulmonologist who has metastatic pancreatic cancer.

item.title: Trump Picks Nicole Saphier for Surgeon General After Withdrawing Casey Means Nomination
item.description: Dr. Means’s nomination had stalled in part over her views on vaccines. The president said he was instead nominating Dr. Nicole B. Saphier, a radiologist at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg and Dani Blum
item.media:credit: Alex Kent for The New York Times
item.media:description: The withdrawal of Dr. Casey Means’s nomination is bound to be a deep disappointment for the Make America Healthy Again movement.

item.title: The Vaccine Skeptic in Trump’s New C.D.C. Leadership Team
item.description: Dr. Sara Brenner is a physician, an F.D.A. official and a “MAHA mom” who has said people should not reflexively believe in the benefits of vaccines.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz/Reuters
item.media:description: Dr. Sara Brenner fully embraced the Make America Healthy Again movement after being named the acting commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration, signing her first emails to the agency’s staff with the slogan.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Joy to Your Day
item.description: Showering at the airport, blossoms in seams and the bar for joy.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Scientists Unveil ‘Long Lost’ Map for Smell
item.description: The odor receptors in the nose are not distributed at random but organized in a precise spatial pattern, two new studies reveal.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: Readers Share Stories on How to Prepare for a Longer Life
item.description: We asked Times readers to share their best advice for a safe, satisfying and financially comfortable life — no matter how old you are or how long you live.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Oliver Parini for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rae S. Earley, 25, is a patient service specialist who lives in Burlington, Vt.

item.title: Hoping the Last Check Bounces … and Other Financial Approaches to a Long Life
item.description: Americans are increasingly dealing with a dilemma: How to balance a long life with having enough money to live it happily.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Rosen
item.media:credit: Dadu Shin

item.title: Facebook Has a Health Scam Problem
item.description: A new report found hundreds of thousands of scam ads for medical products, some of which were illegal or had been deemed dangerous.
item.dc:creator: Teddy Rosenbluth
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times

item.title: Can Spending More Improve Your Health and Prolong Your Life?
item.description: The desire to live longer and healthier lives has spawned a growth in services and supplements. So what works best?
item.dc:creator: Paul Sullivan
item.media:credit: Dadu Shin

item.title: Thanks to GLP-1s, Obesity Experts Are Trying to Understand ‘Food Noise’
item.description: Before the rise of GLP-1s, obesity experts didn’t study the internal buzz that compels people to eat. Now that food noise is being switched off, they want to understand it.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Xia Gordon

item.title: Cellular Rejuvenation Has the Potential to Reverse Aging
item.description: A new therapy has the potential to cure hundreds of diseases — and even reverse aging.
item.dc:creator: Susan Dominus

item.title: Pregnancy With Lupus Is Risky. Would She Be Able to Carry Her Baby to Term?
item.description: Fatimah Shepherd’s kidneys were compromised, and pregnancy could send her into kidney failure.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin and Heather Khalifa
item.media:credit: Heather Khalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fatimah Shepherd, a New York City Fire Department dispatcher, lives with lupus, an autoimmune disease that wreaks havoc on the kidneys and disproportionately affects Black, Hispanic and Asian women.

item.title: Paddling the Threatened Boundary Waters
item.description: A proposed copper mine in northern Minnesota has become a battleground for politicians and environmentalists — and a pressing reason to explore the waterways.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Pearson
item.media:credit: Aaron Lavinsky/Star Tribune, via Getty Images

item.title: A Full-Body Workout You Can Do In the Park
item.description: All you need for this routine is a bench and a patch of grass.
item.dc:creator: Alyssa Ages and Theodore Tae
item.media:credit: Theodore Tae/The New York Times
